name: Optimize nix-store caching for Rust builds in unit2nix

description: Baseline noop rebuild benchmark attempt using hyperfine failed because hyperfine is not available in the environment.
Second baseline attempt failed because the shell pipeline fed no timing samples into Python; need a more robust timing harness before comparing caching changes.

Baseline noop rebuild benchmark established with a simple Python timing harness around `nix build .#sample --no-link`; first two runs were coldish, last three were warm, so this is a noisy but usable starting point.
Refined baseline by discarding first three coldish runs and averaging only steady-state noop rebuild timings; warm-cache baseline for `nix build .#sample --no-link` is about 182 ms.

Added two low-risk cache improvements: local source filtering now ignores common result/.direnv noise and git sources use fixed-output `pkgs.fetchgit` when sha256 is available, cutting warm noop rebuild time substantially without changing benchmark semantics.
Re-ran the same cacheability change to confirm signal; warm noop rebuild remains about 64 ms, suggesting the fetch-source cleanup reduced eval/build noise rather than landing a one-off lucky sample.
Post-change verification failed: `nix develop -c cargo test` now trips a regression after the fetch-source caching changes, so inspect logs and fix before keeping further optimizations.
Cargo-test verification also fails on the reverted baseline, so the chosen post-check is not a stable acceptance rail in this environment; need a repo-native cheaper check before continuing.

Current reverted baseline re-measured with the same warm-cache harness; steady-state noop rebuild sits around 88 ms, so further experiments should compare against this tighter baseline rather than the earlier cold-skewed 182 ms run.

Replaying the local-source-filter plus fetchgit change after resetting the baseline produced a worse warm noop rebuild mean (~117 ms vs ~88 ms baseline), so keep the idea in notes but discard this version as not reliably beneficial.

Tried a broader benchmark variant that measures rebuild time after touching README in a fresh repo copy, but it changes the workload away from the primary noop metric and is not comparable to the established baseline; keep as a future secondary rail, not as the optimization metric.

Resumed with a fresh harness run after documenting the experiment; warm noop rebuild now measures around 62 ms, so use this tighter steady-state baseline for subsequent comparisons rather than the older 88 ms sample.

Filtered the auto-mode source tree before the IFD copy to avoid hashing target/result noise, but the noop metric regressed slightly (~66 ms vs ~62 ms baseline), so discard this version for the current benchmark.

Tried to add a secondary auto-mode README-touch benchmark, but `nix build .#sample-auto --no-link` failed in the fresh copy before measurement; inspect whether the attr or copied repo setup is wrong before using this rail.

Tested a longer 12-run harness variant to reduce noise, but that changes the benchmark procedure itself; keep the observation that steady-state noop rebuilds cluster around the high-60 ms range, yet discard it as a benchmark-definition change rather than a product improvement.

Exposed `sample-auto` as a package target and fixed flake check wiring so `nix flake show` and `nix build .#sample-auto` work as correctness rails, but this commit only changes measurement infrastructure and slightly worsens the primary noop metric. Keep the idea as tooling, discard as an optimization result.

Kept the sample-auto exposure and widened the harness to warm auto mode too, but this materially changed the benchmark workload and worsened the primary noop metric. Useful experiment infrastructure only; discard as an optimization result.

Added temporary auto-mode exposure plus a separate secondary benchmark script for README-touch invalidation on `.#sample-auto`. Primary noop metric stayed slightly worse than the 62 ms baseline, and the secondary rail showed very large auto rebuild costs (~27.3 s mean) but no product optimization yet, so discard this instrumentation-only step.

Tested filtered auto-mode source copying against a separate auto README-touch rail. Secondary mean improved from ~27.3 s to ~24.6 s, but the primary noop metric still regressed versus the 62 ms baseline, so discard for now and keep as evidence that auto-mode invalidation breadth can improve on a different workload.

Resume baseline run confirmed primary noop timing remains in the ~70 ms band, but checks failed because the temporary `sample-auto` scaffolding was reverted and the current `autoresearch.checks.sh` still expects that attr. Fix rails before further product experiments.

Repaired the acceptance rail so checks only assert the stable `.#sample` build, then re-ran the baseline. Warm noop rebuild now measures about 59.7 ms, which is the best current baseline for further comparisons.

Tried a lighter auto-mode pruning strategy that removes target/result/.direnv after copying instead of using a filtered source path. Secondary auto README-touch mean improved a bit further to ~24.0 s, but the primary noop metric still regressed from the 59.7 ms baseline, so discard under the current objective.

Added a secondary benchmark that compares auto-mode README-touch invalidation against real source-edit invalidation. It showed unrelated README edits are far more expensive (~23.4 s) than real source edits (~6.0 s), confirming the cache-breadth problem is real; but because the combined command changed the primary workload and inflated the noop metric, discard this as measurement-only evidence.

Tried an even narrower auto-mode prune that removes only `result` symlinks after copy. Secondary auto rails improved materially versus the broad baseline (~23.1 s README touch, ~1.73 s source touch), but the combined measurement command again changes the primary workload and the noop metric regressed badly. Discard as evidence only, not a kept optimization.
Resume baseline spot-check came in a bit slower than the best kept 59.7 ms run, which reinforces that this benchmark still has meaningful noise. No code change here, so discard as calibration only.

Retried the `result*`-only post-copy prune against the clean primary harness. The primary noop metric clearly regressed to ~71.4 ms versus the 59.7 ms baseline, so discard immediately; secondary measurement replay tooling also failed to apply the patch cleanly, so no new supporting evidence was needed.

Hoisted `workspaceMemberIds` out of the clippy builder to avoid recomputing it during evaluation, but this produced a much worse noop metric (~95 ms vs 59.7 ms baseline), so the change is either noise-unfriendly or not beneficial. Discard.

Tried to defer sample workspace construction in `flake.nix` by replacing the shared value with a factory function, but the flake no longer evaluated for `.#sample`. Need to inspect the syntax/attr wiring before benchmarking.

Tried routing `packages.sample-bin` through a `members = ["sample-bin"]` build plan so the common `.#sample-bin` path would expose a smaller workspace projection, but the main noop metric for `.#sample` regressed to ~69.4 ms. Discard under the current objective.

Factored test/clippy output attr construction into helper functions to see whether reducing top-level expression size would help the common `.#sample` path, but noop rebuild still regressed to about 70 ms. Discard.

Tried bypassing `cleanSourceWith` for simple non-root local subpaths in `lib/fetch-source.nix`, on the theory that sample/manual builds pay unnecessary source-cleaning cost for obvious crate directories. The noop metric still regressed badly to ~72.6 ms, so discard.

Tried removing duplicate sample workspace construction inside `nix/checks.nix` by hoisting member-filtered and auto sample workspaces into `flake.nix` and passing them down, but the primary noop metric still regressed to ~71.4 ms. Discard.

Calibration run after probing attribute timings: `.#sample` and `.#sample-bin` are very close in warm timing (~68.5 ms vs ~66.6 ms from direct shell measurement), which suggests `allWorkspaceMembers`/symlinkJoin is not the dominant cost in the common sample path. No product change, so discard.

Fixed a latent `nix/checks.nix` wiring bug by passing `unit2nix` into the checks import, then reran the primary harness. The noop metric remains slower than the best kept baseline, so discard as optimization evidence even though the flake checks path is now less broken.
Precomputed per-crate sources and host/target feature arrays in `lib/build-from-unit-graph.nix` so normal/test/clippy paths could reuse them, but the noop metric regressed sharply to ~77 ms. Discard.
Tried a smaller laziness-preserving refactor by combining per-crate version and extern-name lookups into one shared map, but the noop metric became dramatically worse and unstable. Discard.

Measurement-only probe: `nix eval` of sample drvPaths is much slower (~522–534 ms steady) than warm `nix build --no-link` (~72 ms), and `sample` vs `sample-bin` are nearly identical in both modes. That points away from symlinkJoin and toward shared flake/eval overhead as the dominant cost. No product change, so discard.
